title: Acute Coronary Syndrome — NSTEMI
oneLiner: Ischaemic myocardial injury without ST-elevation on ECG; common, heterogeneous, requires early risk stratification to guide angiography timing.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **ST-elevation MI (STEMI) misdiagnosed as NSTEMI** – *Single finding:* persistent ST-elevation ≥1mm in contiguous leads on any repeat ECG (do serial ECGs if ongoing pain). Treat as STEMI.
- **Acute aortic dissection** – *Single finding:* sudden “tearing” interscapular pain + pulse/blood pressure differential between arms or new aortic regurgitation murmur. Do not give antiplatelets/anticoagulation until CT aorta done.
- **Type 2 MI due to undiagnosed sepsis/hypotension** – *Single finding:* low blood pressure, lactate >2 mmol/L, fever, or focal infection source. Treat underlying cause first; antiplatelets may worsen bleeding risk.
- **Mechanical complication (papillary muscle rupture, VSD, free wall rupture)** – *Single finding:* new pansystolic murmur + pulmonary oedema or cardiogenic shock after 24-48h of NSTEMI. Emergency echo; surgical repair needed.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Central chest pain or pressure (often dull, heavy, or burning) lasting >20 min, may radiate to left arm, jaw, neck, or back – but can be atypical (epigastric, isolated dyspnoea, or silent in elderly/diabetics)
  - Dyspnoea, diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting, presyncope/syncope
  - “Angina equivalent” in women/diabetes: fatigue, indigestion, breathlessness
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Vital signs: may be normal; tachycardia, bradycardia, hypertension, or hypotension possible
  - Signs of heart failure (lung crepitations, raised JVP, peripheral oedema)
  - New murmur (mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle ischaemia) or S3 gallop
  - No focal neurological deficit (unless concurrent stroke/embolism)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - **Unstable angina** – Similar pain but no troponin rise (NSTEMI requires elevated troponin)
  - **Type 2 MI (demand ischaemia)** – Troponin rise due to tachycardia, hypotension, anaemia, or sepsis; no acute coronary thrombosis. Differentiate by triggers, not angiogram.
  - **Pericarditis** – Sharp, positional pain, relieved by leaning forward; widespread ST-elevation on ECG, PR depression; pericardial rub.
  - **Myocarditis** – Often young, viral prodrome, troponin rise, diffuse myocardial oedema on MRI
  - **Pulmonary embolism** – Dyspnoea out of proportion to chest pain; hypoxia, raised D-dimer, RV strain on echo/CTPA
  - **Aortic dissection** – Severe hypertension, pulse deficit, widened mediastinum on CXR
  - **Oesophageal rupture (Boerhaave)** – Vomiting, surgical emphysema, pleural effusion
- **Splitter:** *Persistent ST-elevation (even if minor) or new LBBB + chest pain → STEMI, not NSTEMI.* Immediate cath lab activation.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - 12-lead ECG within 10 min of arrival – repeat if pain changes or persists (look for new ST-elevation, LBBB, or dynamic T-wave inversion/ST depression)
  - Continuous cardiac monitoring (arrhythmia detection)
  - Blood pressure both arms (if pulse deficit → consider dissection)
  - Oxygen saturation (target ≥94% if hypoxic)
- **Bloods:**
  - High-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTnI or T) – serial: on arrival and at 3h (or 6h if low-risk and 3h result inconclusive)
  - Full blood count, U&Es, creatinine, eGFR, glucose, lipids (fasting later)
  - Coagulation screen (before anticoagulation)
  - Consider BNP/NT-proBNP if heart failure suspected
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray** – rule out aortic widening, pneumothorax, signs of heart failure
  - **Transthoracic echocardiogram** – within 24-48h (assess LV function, wall motion abnormalities, valve disease, exclude mechanical complication)
  - **Coronary angiography** – timing based on risk score (see below)
- **Special tests:**
  - CT coronary angiogram – alternative for non-obstructive disease or if low-intermediate risk with non-diagnostic ECG
  - Cardiac MRI – for myocarditis or unclear diagnosis
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Universal Definition of MI (4th edition):** hs-cTn rise/fall + at least one of: ischaemic symptoms, new ischaemic ECG changes, pathological Q waves, imaging evidence of new wall motion abnormality, or identification of coronary thrombus
  - **GRACE 2.0 score** – online calculator (or mobile app); uses age, HR, SBP, Cr, Killip class, cardiac arrest at admission, ST deviation, elevated cardiac enzymes. Stratifies into:
    - Low risk (<140 points): conservative management or angiography within 72h
    - Intermediate (140-154): angiography within 72h
    - High (>154): angiography within 24h
- **Never-miss test:** **Repeat ECG within 30 min if pain ongoing** – missing evolving STEMI is a serious error.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Oxygen only if SpO2 <94% (avoid hyperoxia)
  - Morphine 2.5-5 mg IV (if severe pain) + antiemetic (e.g., metoclopramide 10 mg IV)
  - Aspirin 300 mg (chewed or crushed) – unless allergy/active bleeding
  - Ticagrelor 180 mg loading dose (or clopidogrel 300-600 mg if ticagrelor contraindicated)
  - Anticoagulation: fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC daily (if CrCl ≥20) – or enoxaparin/UFH if PCI imminent or low bleeding risk (check local policy)
  - Nitrates: GTN sublingual or IV infusion if ongoing ischaemia and no hypotension (25-50 µg/min IV, titrate)
  - Beta-blocker (oral): metoprolol 25-50 mg PO if HR >60, SBP >100, and no heart failure/AV block – consider early (within 24h) but avoid in acute pulmonary oedema or cardiogenic shock
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Serial troponin (repeat at 3h; if negative and low GRACE, consider stress echo or CTCA)
  - Admit to coronary care unit or monitored bed (high-risk) – or cardiology ward with telemetry (low-risk)
  - Start dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT): aspirin 75 mg OD + ticagrelor 90 mg BD (or clopidogrel 75 mg OD)
  - Anticoagulation: continue fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC OD (or enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BD SC if PCI planned) – stop after 48h or after angiogram if no stent/need for further therapy
  - High-dose statin: atorvastatin 80 mg OD (start on admission, regardless of baseline cholesterol)
  - ACE inhibitor (e.g., ramipril 2.5 mg BD, titrate) – start within 24-48h if no contraindication (especially if LV dysfunction or anterior MI)
  - Consider eplerenone 25 mg OD (if LVEF ≤40% and already on ACEi + beta-blocker, K+ <5.0)
  - Lifestyle counselling: smoking cessation, diet, cardiac rehab referral
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - DAPT for 12 months (aspirin + ticagrelor/clopidogrel) – then lifelong aspirin
  - Statin: atorvastatin 80 mg OD (or rosuvastatin if intolerant)
  - Beta-blocker: e.g., bisoprolol 5-10 mg OD (long-term if LVEF ≤40% or post-MI)
  - ACEi/ARB: continue indefinitely (ramipril 10 mg OD target)
  - Aldosterone antagonist: eplerenone 25-50 mg OD (if LVEF ≤40% after 7 days)
  - Cardiac rehabilitation programme
  - Risk factor control: BP <130/80, LDL <1.4 mmol/L, HbA1c <53 mmol/mol (if diabetic)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: nil unless hypotensive – avoid fluid overload (no routine IV fluids, keep euvolemic)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if low bleeding risk, CrCl ≥30) – note: may overlap with therapeutic anticoagulation (fondaparinux); discuss with seniors
  - Diet: normal ward diet (no NBM unless urgent angiography planned in morning)
  - Observations: NEWS2 q15min initially, then q1-2h once stable; cardiac monitoring continuous for ≥24h
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Aspirin 300 mg PO stat (chewed), then 75 mg OD long-term
  - Ticagrelor 180 mg PO stat, then 90 mg BD (for 12 months)
  - Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC OD (start after loading, continue ≤48h) – [check if CrCl <20]
  - Atorvastatin 80 mg OD (start on admission)
  - Ramipril 2.5 mg BD, titrate to 10 mg OD (start within 24-48h if SBP >100, no hyperkalaemia, no renal failure)
  - Bisoprolol 5 mg OD (start if stable, no acute HF, HR >60)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Clopidogrel 300-600 mg loading then 75 mg OD (if ticagrelor CI or aspirin allergy)
  - Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BD SC (if PCI planned within 24h or fondaparinux not available)
  - Unfractionated heparin IV (UFH) – loading 60 U/kg (max 4000 U) then infusion 12 U/kg/h (monitor aPTT) – if urgent PCI within 2h
  - GTN IV infusion: 25-50 µg/min initially, titrate to pain relief up to 200 µg/min (avoid in RV infarct, severe bradycardia, hypotension)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Ticagrelor + aspirin: increased bleeding risk (avoid NSAIDs, anticoagulants with caution)
  - Fondaparinux: avoid if CrCl <20 mL/min; use UFH instead
  - Statins: avoid with fibrates (risk of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis); check LFTs
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Fondaparinux: avoid if CrCl <20 mL/min (use UFH)
  - Enoxaparin: prophylaxis dose 40 mg OD safe if CrCl ≥30; if <30, use 20 mg OD SC (therapeutic dosing requires dose reduction)
  - Ticagrelor: no dose adjustment; caution in severe hepatic disease
  - Atorvastatin: caution in severe hepatic impairment; no renal adjustment needed
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC OD is prophylactic dose – DO NOT give therapeutic dose (e.g. 5 mg) unless specified; confusion with treatment-dose enoxaparin is common. Check product and be aware that fondaparinux 2.5 mg is FDA/UK-approved for NSTEMI (therapeutic anticoagulation) – but dose is still 2.5 mg, not 10 mg. No reversal agent.**
  - **Ticagrelor – loading dose 180 mg (not 90 mg). Missing loading dose risks stent thrombosis.**
  - **Enoxaparin – check the strength (100 mg/mL vs 20 mg/mL syringes). Use correct pre-filled syringe for dose (e.g., 1 mg/kg requires 0.01 mL/kg) – calculate carefully.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Arrhythmias: ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation, atrial fibrillation, heart block (especially inferior MI)
  - Cardiogenic shock (LV failure)
  - Mechanical complications (papillary muscle rupture, VSD, free wall rupture) – typically days 2-7
  - Recurrent ischaemia/infarction
  - Bleeding (from DAPT, anticoagulation, access site)
  - Contrast-induced acute kidney injury (if angiography)
- **Chronic:**
  - Heart failure (reduced or preserved LVEF)
  - Left ventricular aneurysm or thrombus (especially anterior STEMI, but can occur in NSTEMI with extensive involvement)
  - Post-MI pericarditis (Dressler syndrome) – weeks later, treat with colchicine/NSAIDs (but avoid NSAIDs if on DAPT? Use colchicine)
  - Recurrent angina or need for revascularisation
sections.Prognosis:
- 30-day mortality for NSTEMI ~5-10% (higher if high GRACE, age >75, heart failure, diabetes); long-term mortality ~15-20% at 1 year.
- Risk of recurrent MI or revascularisation is significant; early revascularisation reduces major adverse cardiac events (MACE) in high-risk patients.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Calculate GRACE 2.0 score on admission** to decide timing of angiography – this decision is more important than drug choices.
- **Start high-dose statin immediately** – atorvastatin 80 mg reduces MACE independent of cholesterol lowering.
- **Check LV function (echo) within 24-48h** – drives need for ACEi, beta-blocker, and eplerenone. If LVEF ≤40%, start eplerenone after 7 days.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay angiography in high-GRACE NSTEMI** waiting for troponin to peak – guidelines say <24h (high), <72h (intermediate). Low-risk can be conservative.
- **Do not give fondaparinux if CrCl <20 mL/min** – use UFH or enoxaparin (with monitoring). Fondaparinux has no reversal agent, so bleeding risk high.
- **Do not withhold beta-blocker in acute heart failure or cardiogenic shock** – can worsen pulmonary oedema. Start only when euvolaemic and BP >100 systolic.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Recurrent or worsening chest pain (especially at rest)
  - New shortness of breath, palpitations, or syncope
  - Signs of bleeding (easy bruising, dark stools, haemoptysis, severe headache – suggests intracranial bleed)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Cardiology clinic within 4-6 weeks
  - Cardiac rehabilitation referral before discharge or within 4 weeks
  - GP review for risk factor management and DAPT review at 12 months
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/registrar immediately if:**
  - Chest pain persists despite GTN and morphine – need repeat ECG, consider STEMI activation
  - New hypotension (SBP <90), pulmonary oedema, or signs of cardiogenic shock
  - Heart rate >120 or <40, new murmur, or signs of tamponade (raised JVP, pulsus paradoxus)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or single parameter of concern (bradycardia, hypoxia, hypotension)
- **ICU/CCU criteria:**
  - Shock (inotropic support required)
  - Recurrent ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation
  - Mechanical complication requiring urgent echo/surgical review
  - High-risk NSTEMI with ongoing ischaemia needing urgent catheterisation

rawMarkdown:
# Acute Coronary Syndrome — NSTEMI

**One-liner:** Ischaemic myocardial injury without ST-elevation on ECG; common, heterogeneous, requires early risk stratification to guide angiography timing.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **ST-elevation MI (STEMI) misdiagnosed as NSTEMI** – *Single finding:* persistent ST-elevation ≥1mm in contiguous leads on any repeat ECG (do serial ECGs if ongoing pain). Treat as STEMI.
- **Acute aortic dissection** – *Single finding:* sudden “tearing” interscapular pain + pulse/blood pressure differential between arms or new aortic regurgitation murmur. Do not give antiplatelets/anticoagulation until CT aorta done.
- **Type 2 MI due to undiagnosed sepsis/hypotension** – *Single finding:* low blood pressure, lactate >2 mmol/L, fever, or focal infection source. Treat underlying cause first; antiplatelets may worsen bleeding risk.
- **Mechanical complication (papillary muscle rupture, VSD, free wall rupture)** – *Single finding:* new pansystolic murmur + pulmonary oedema or cardiogenic shock after 24-48h of NSTEMI. Emergency echo; surgical repair needed.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Central chest pain or pressure (often dull, heavy, or burning) lasting >20 min, may radiate to left arm, jaw, neck, or back – but can be atypical (epigastric, isolated dyspnoea, or silent in elderly/diabetics)
  - Dyspnoea, diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting, presyncope/syncope
  - “Angina equivalent” in women/diabetes: fatigue, indigestion, breathlessness
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Vital signs: may be normal; tachycardia, bradycardia, hypertension, or hypotension possible
  - Signs of heart failure (lung crepitations, raised JVP, peripheral oedema)
  - New murmur (mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle ischaemia) or S3 gallop
  - No focal neurological deficit (unless concurrent stroke/embolism)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - **Unstable angina** – Similar pain but no troponin rise (NSTEMI requires elevated troponin)
  - **Type 2 MI (demand ischaemia)** – Troponin rise due to tachycardia, hypotension, anaemia, or sepsis; no acute coronary thrombosis. Differentiate by triggers, not angiogram.
  - **Pericarditis** – Sharp, positional pain, relieved by leaning forward; widespread ST-elevation on ECG, PR depression; pericardial rub.
  - **Myocarditis** – Often young, viral prodrome, troponin rise, diffuse myocardial oedema on MRI
  - **Pulmonary embolism** – Dyspnoea out of proportion to chest pain; hypoxia, raised D-dimer, RV strain on echo/CTPA
  - **Aortic dissection** – Severe hypertension, pulse deficit, widened mediastinum on CXR
  - **Oesophageal rupture (Boerhaave)** – Vomiting, surgical emphysema, pleural effusion
- **Splitter:** *Persistent ST-elevation (even if minor) or new LBBB + chest pain → STEMI, not NSTEMI.* Immediate cath lab activation.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - 12-lead ECG within 10 min of arrival – repeat if pain changes or persists (look for new ST-elevation, LBBB, or dynamic T-wave inversion/ST depression)
  - Continuous cardiac monitoring (arrhythmia detection)
  - Blood pressure both arms (if pulse deficit → consider dissection)
  - Oxygen saturation (target ≥94% if hypoxic)
- **Bloods:**
  - High-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTnI or T) – serial: on arrival and at 3h (or 6h if low-risk and 3h result inconclusive)
  - Full blood count, U&Es, creatinine, eGFR, glucose, lipids (fasting later)
  - Coagulation screen (before anticoagulation)
  - Consider BNP/NT-proBNP if heart failure suspected
- **Imaging:**
  - **Chest X-ray** – rule out aortic widening, pneumothorax, signs of heart failure
  - **Transthoracic echocardiogram** – within 24-48h (assess LV function, wall motion abnormalities, valve disease, exclude mechanical complication)
  - **Coronary angiography** – timing based on risk score (see below)
- **Special tests:**
  - CT coronary angiogram – alternative for non-obstructive disease or if low-intermediate risk with non-diagnostic ECG
  - Cardiac MRI – for myocarditis or unclear diagnosis
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Universal Definition of MI (4th edition):** hs-cTn rise/fall + at least one of: ischaemic symptoms, new ischaemic ECG changes, pathological Q waves, imaging evidence of new wall motion abnormality, or identification of coronary thrombus
  - **GRACE 2.0 score** – online calculator (or mobile app); uses age, HR, SBP, Cr, Killip class, cardiac arrest at admission, ST deviation, elevated cardiac enzymes. Stratifies into:
    - Low risk (<140 points): conservative management or angiography within 72h
    - Intermediate (140-154): angiography within 72h
    - High (>154): angiography within 24h
- **Never-miss test:** **Repeat ECG within 30 min if pain ongoing** – missing evolving STEMI is a serious error.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Oxygen only if SpO2 <94% (avoid hyperoxia)
  - Morphine 2.5-5 mg IV (if severe pain) + antiemetic (e.g., metoclopramide 10 mg IV)
  - Aspirin 300 mg (chewed or crushed) – unless allergy/active bleeding
  - Ticagrelor 180 mg loading dose (or clopidogrel 300-600 mg if ticagrelor contraindicated)
  - Anticoagulation: fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC daily (if CrCl ≥20) – or enoxaparin/UFH if PCI imminent or low bleeding risk (check local policy)
  - Nitrates: GTN sublingual or IV infusion if ongoing ischaemia and no hypotension (25-50 µg/min IV, titrate)
  - Beta-blocker (oral): metoprolol 25-50 mg PO if HR >60, SBP >100, and no heart failure/AV block – consider early (within 24h) but avoid in acute pulmonary oedema or cardiogenic shock
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Serial troponin (repeat at 3h; if negative and low GRACE, consider stress echo or CTCA)
  - Admit to coronary care unit or monitored bed (high-risk) – or cardiology ward with telemetry (low-risk)
  - Start dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT): aspirin 75 mg OD + ticagrelor 90 mg BD (or clopidogrel 75 mg OD)
  - Anticoagulation: continue fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC OD (or enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BD SC if PCI planned) – stop after 48h or after angiogram if no stent/need for further therapy
  - High-dose statin: atorvastatin 80 mg OD (start on admission, regardless of baseline cholesterol)
  - ACE inhibitor (e.g., ramipril 2.5 mg BD, titrate) – start within 24-48h if no contraindication (especially if LV dysfunction or anterior MI)
  - Consider eplerenone 25 mg OD (if LVEF ≤40% and already on ACEi + beta-blocker, K+ <5.0)
  - Lifestyle counselling: smoking cessation, diet, cardiac rehab referral
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - DAPT for 12 months (aspirin + ticagrelor/clopidogrel) – then lifelong aspirin
  - Statin: atorvastatin 80 mg OD (or rosuvastatin if intolerant)
  - Beta-blocker: e.g., bisoprolol 5-10 mg OD (long-term if LVEF ≤40% or post-MI)
  - ACEi/ARB: continue indefinitely (ramipril 10 mg OD target)
  - Aldosterone antagonist: eplerenone 25-50 mg OD (if LVEF ≤40% after 7 days)
  - Cardiac rehabilitation programme
  - Risk factor control: BP <130/80, LDL <1.4 mmol/L, HbA1c <53 mmol/mol (if diabetic)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: nil unless hypotensive – avoid fluid overload (no routine IV fluids, keep euvolemic)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if low bleeding risk, CrCl ≥30) – note: may overlap with therapeutic anticoagulation (fondaparinux); discuss with seniors
  - Diet: normal ward diet (no NBM unless urgent angiography planned in morning)
  - Observations: NEWS2 q15min initially, then q1-2h once stable; cardiac monitoring continuous for ≥24h

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Aspirin 300 mg PO stat (chewed), then 75 mg OD long-term
  - Ticagrelor 180 mg PO stat, then 90 mg BD (for 12 months)
  - Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC OD (start after loading, continue ≤48h) – [check if CrCl <20]
  - Atorvastatin 80 mg OD (start on admission)
  - Ramipril 2.5 mg BD, titrate to 10 mg OD (start within 24-48h if SBP >100, no hyperkalaemia, no renal failure)
  - Bisoprolol 5 mg OD (start if stable, no acute HF, HR >60)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Clopidogrel 300-600 mg loading then 75 mg OD (if ticagrelor CI or aspirin allergy)
  - Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BD SC (if PCI planned within 24h or fondaparinux not available)
  - Unfractionated heparin IV (UFH) – loading 60 U/kg (max 4000 U) then infusion 12 U/kg/h (monitor aPTT) – if urgent PCI within 2h
  - GTN IV infusion: 25-50 µg/min initially, titrate to pain relief up to 200 µg/min (avoid in RV infarct, severe bradycardia, hypotension)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Ticagrelor + aspirin: increased bleeding risk (avoid NSAIDs, anticoagulants with caution)
  - Fondaparinux: avoid if CrCl <20 mL/min; use UFH instead
  - Statins: avoid with fibrates (risk of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis); check LFTs
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Fondaparinux: avoid if CrCl <20 mL/min (use UFH)
  - Enoxaparin: prophylaxis dose 40 mg OD safe if CrCl ≥30; if <30, use 20 mg OD SC (therapeutic dosing requires dose reduction)
  - Ticagrelor: no dose adjustment; caution in severe hepatic disease
  - Atorvastatin: caution in severe hepatic impairment; no renal adjustment needed
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC OD is prophylactic dose – DO NOT give therapeutic dose (e.g. 5 mg) unless specified; confusion with treatment-dose enoxaparin is common. Check product and be aware that fondaparinux 2.5 mg is FDA/UK-approved for NSTEMI (therapeutic anticoagulation) – but dose is still 2.5 mg, not 10 mg. No reversal agent.**
  - **Ticagrelor – loading dose 180 mg (not 90 mg). Missing loading dose risks stent thrombosis.**
  - **Enoxaparin – check the strength (100 mg/mL vs 20 mg/mL syringes). Use correct pre-filled syringe for dose (e.g., 1 mg/kg requires 0.01 mL/kg) – calculate carefully.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Arrhythmias: ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation, atrial fibrillation, heart block (especially inferior MI)
  - Cardiogenic shock (LV failure)
  - Mechanical complications (papillary muscle rupture, VSD, free wall rupture) – typically days 2-7
  - Recurrent ischaemia/infarction
  - Bleeding (from DAPT, anticoagulation, access site)
  - Contrast-induced acute kidney injury (if angiography)
- **Chronic:**
  - Heart failure (reduced or preserved LVEF)
  - Left ventricular aneurysm or thrombus (especially anterior STEMI, but can occur in NSTEMI with extensive involvement)
  - Post-MI pericarditis (Dressler syndrome) – weeks later, treat with colchicine/NSAIDs (but avoid NSAIDs if on DAPT? Use colchicine)
  - Recurrent angina or need for revascularisation

### Prognosis
- 30-day mortality for NSTEMI ~5-10% (higher if high GRACE, age >75, heart failure, diabetes); long-term mortality ~15-20% at 1 year.
- Risk of recurrent MI or revascularisation is significant; early revascularisation reduces major adverse cardiac events (MACE) in high-risk patients.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Calculate GRACE 2.0 score on admission** to decide timing of angiography – this decision is more important than drug choices.
- **Start high-dose statin immediately** – atorvastatin 80 mg reduces MACE independent of cholesterol lowering.
- **Check LV function (echo) within 24-48h** – drives need for ACEi, beta-blocker, and eplerenone. If LVEF ≤40%, start eplerenone after 7 days.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay angiography in high-GRACE NSTEMI** waiting for troponin to peak – guidelines say <24h (high), <72h (intermediate). Low-risk can be conservative.
- **Do not give fondaparinux if CrCl <20 mL/min** – use UFH or enoxaparin (with monitoring). Fondaparinux has no reversal agent, so bleeding risk high.
- **Do not withhold beta-blocker in acute heart failure or cardiogenic shock** – can worsen pulmonary oedema. Start only when euvolaemic and BP >100 systolic.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Recurrent or worsening chest pain (especially at rest)
  - New shortness of breath, palpitations, or syncope
  - Signs of bleeding (easy bruising, dark stools, haemoptysis, severe headache – suggests intracranial bleed)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Cardiology clinic within 4-6 weeks
  - Cardiac rehabilitation referral before discharge or within 4 weeks
  - GP review for risk factor management and DAPT review at 12 months

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/registrar immediately if:**
  - Chest pain persists despite GTN and morphine – need repeat ECG, consider STEMI activation
  - New hypotension (SBP <90), pulmonary oedema, or signs of cardiogenic shock
  - Heart rate >120 or <40, new murmur, or signs of tamponade (raised JVP, pulsus paradoxus)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or single parameter of concern (bradycardia, hypoxia, hypotension)
- **ICU/CCU criteria:**
  - Shock (inotropic support required)
  - Recurrent ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation
  - Mechanical complication requiring urgent echo/surgical review
  - High-risk NSTEMI with ongoing ischaemia needing urgent catheterisation
